# Transcrição: 02- Course 5 (Lesson 1) - Working With Commercial Brokers To Find Deals

## [00:00 - 03:03]

Working with commercial brokers to find deals, I strongly advise that you work with a broker. I see so many real estate investors, the first thing they do is they go out and get their real estate license and say, I said, why are you doing that? I don't understand. They're like, oh, because I want to save on the broker fees. I'm like, dude, that's dumb. If you can't pay a broker to find you a deal and help you close a deal, if you're trying to save a point and a half or two points or three points to buy a deal, that deal doesn't make sense anyway. Look, the first step to finding a deal is finding a deal, and you have to know where to look. Your job is not to figure out how to save fees. Your job is to figure out how to find deals. Many real estate investors, myself included, will use things like LoopNet or the paper or advertisements. This is a free service that boasts more than 500,000 listings from coast to coast, but it is not the best place to find deals. I'm going to give you and include in here a complete list of every major brokerage house in the United States and the names of the people you need to talk to. In every market, there's two major brokers, two winners in that market, Max Four that can help you find deals. Can you find a deal on LoopNet? Yes, possibly. It's a free service. You can look at deals there. You might find a deal there, but you're more likely to find deals that nobody else wanted on LoopNet. What you're sure to find on LoopNet, though, is the names of brokers with access to other deals. There's a lot better uses of LoopNet than just trying to find a deal. What you're trying to find is access to brokers and agents. The brokers and the agents, like the lenders, are your friends, not your enemies. You have to create relationships with them. Use LoopNet to find the names of commercial agents in your market and brokers in that given market. Hone in on the area you want to research and see which brokers pop to the top of the list. Then pick up the phone and make a call. Look, by the way, you don't have to be interested in that specific property they have listed on LoopNet, although you certainly might be. Use the contact information on the LoopNet listing page, call or email that contact, and start vetting your process. However, your best bet for finding top-tier real estate in any area, in any class, A, B, and C, and even D, is to identify the premier three or four brokers in your market and make contact. These are the guys and gals that have the deals. If you have them, if you know them, if you have a relationship with them, if they trust you, you will find great investments and great opportunities. No matter the market and no matter the economic conditions, you want to use your broker. That's why at Cardone Capital, we have such an advantage in the market because I'm known.

## [03:03 - 06:08]

25 years of buying deals, 25 years of getting deals done, 25 years, 5,000 apartments, $1 billion of assets that I've done deals on. These guys know me. They've heard of me. I have a track record. You're going to have to either build that, sell that, or buy that. What do I mean by that? You're going to have to build a portfolio of experience so you can tell that broker, I'm a real deal. I'm not wasting your time. We have the money. We have the funding. We have the investors. We have the know-how. We have the financing. We do deals. Or, you're going to have to sell it. You're going to have to sell your story. You're going to have to sell why you're the guy to do the deal and how you're going to bring it all together. You're going to have to build that relationship. Much harder to do. Or three, number three, you buy it. You basically buy partnership with another company that has a lot of experience. You collaborate with a group like mine, maybe, or another's in your market and take advantage of their history. Quality brokers connect buyers and sellers. They earn a commission for that, by the way. And they deserve their commission. They're underpaid, actually. And when the deal is drive to a close, most often it is the broker that is holding, like glue, the deal together. I recently did a deal in Houston, Texas. It was an off-market deal. I found it without a broker. I contacted the seller without a broker. I found out there would be interest. And then I hired a broker to facilitate. I paid the broker $250,000 to facilitate an $80 million transaction so that I had glue in the deal. Look, and by the way, that broker wasn't even in the market that I was buying in. The best brokers have solid relationships and connections. It's collaboration. It's building your creds up. So on both sides of the market, whether you're a seller or a buyer, you use the broker. They can be effective and very efficient at identifying the right players on both the buy side and the sale side. For investors, this can be a huge win because your broker will know the market and your unique wants and needs. They'll be able to dial you up the second something drops, something becomes available, or somebody falls out of financing on a particular deal and help you cut through the clutter and get your offer in as soon as possible and get you to the top of the stack in many cases of other people that might be making offers. Remember what I said in the earlier courses. You want properties that other people are bidding on. You do not want to be the only guy bidding for a piece of property and that means you need a broker to get you to the top of the list. This is where your sales skills and your relationship skills are going to become vital. This is where you as a closer, not as a buyer, are going to become essential. The broker and the brokerage house can be the difference between you locking down a deal

## [06:08 - 09:11]

of a lifetime or watching it go to the competition. This refers to great brokers, by the way, not hobbyists and by the way, not all brokers are created equal. When working to identify the best brokers in your market, look for brokers who are constantly chasing and securing new listings, not brokers that are sending you out CA's, Confidentiality agreements and saying sign here, protect me from my commission. You want the listing broker on a deal. This is a sign that the broker is actively engaged in the market and trusted by sellers. This is a networking business. The brokerage business is networking sellers and buyers. You want brokers who are constantly out in the marketplace getting listings. Brokers who have access to pocket listings and off market deals is great, but not as good as those who have acquired the listing on a property. Exclusive listings. There are tons of deals on LoopNet and CoStar and other online sites. Those are great, but they also have tons of competition going at them. By the way, when you call a broker with a deal and say, I saw your deal on LoopNet, they automatically think that you're an amateur because that's not where the pros go to find their deals. Some people would suggest that you want the deals that no one else knows about. This is just an old, small idea. It's the old thinking, buy low, sell high. Find deals no one else knows about. Get the deals as they're coming out of bankruptcy. This is all small people giving you advice on maybe accumulating three or four deals. I've done 40 deals, 5,000 units. I study the guys that have 40,000 units, not guys that have put together two or three deals. You want the deals that other people want. Of course there's less competition on a deal that no one else wants, but why does no one else want it? Brokers who are engaged and ready to go in the market with exclusive listings are the best way to go. Now, let me warn you that these brokers at the top of the food chain are not going to call you back and they're not going to email you back. There's going to be four brokers on every listing. Those four brokers work for the same company. It's going to be the senior guy, his junior, and two more juniors. I will leave phone calls and emails with all four of those people knowing probably the bottom guy is more likely to call me back than the top guy if I'm not known in the market. If I'm known in the market and I have history, I'm going straight to the top and then I'll still back it up with the other three juniors. By the way, don't dismiss anybody on that list. They can all help you. Work with someone who wants to win your business, but your job in the beginning as an amateur

## [09:11 - 12:13]

that wants to become a professional, that wants to make a fortune to build a freaking billion dollar business, your job is to win them over. It's not their job to win you over. Don't expect the best in the business to call you back. It's your job to get in front of them. It's your job to build a relationship. It's your job to convince them that you're a buyer for that product that they have a listing on. And this, by the way, will be your most difficult task in all of real estate. I've had people tell me, oh my God, the tenants, the toilets, the termites, the roof problem, the refinance problems, the credit issues. I said, dude, none of that's the problem. The problem is finding a deal in the first place. Your biggest challenge will be getting a broker to simply call you back. Your second biggest problem will be getting a broker to believe you. The third biggest problem will be getting the broker to call you back, believe you, and then assign you a deal. Brokers are immersed in opportunities and deals and buyers. Every day the broker is getting a call from some guy in the Midwest who wants to buy a deal in Miami or in Houston. Brokers are immersed in the data. Also, your local finance debt guy is going to be immersed in data that's great. Again, the name of the game is data. If your broker can't supply that data, go somewhere else. You can get it somewhere else. Things like real capital analytics. There's plenty of software that you can buy today to grab all the data, the historicals, the market boys. You'll have to pay for this. These are the numbers you're going to base your massive financial and business decisions on and the best place to go for that kind of data is not the broker. Get it from them. Get it from the seller. But the best place to go is your lender. Is your debt arm. Make sure that data is accurate and make sure you can get immediate changing access to things like last month's rent roll. Brokers who are well connected. When you're new to the market, your broker will be your lifeline. And as such, you'll be able to connect you to everyone and everything from property managers to surveyors to attorneys, lenders, title clerks, the whole batch. Remember, this is a networking game. You need a broker who comes ready to play. If they don't know you, they will not flow you deals. And if they don't flow you deals, you will not be making deals. Again, LukeNet is a decent place to start. The paper, the internet could be a decent place to start, but that's not going to be where your great deals are. Referrals could also be a great place. Parties could be a great place. Networking parties, family offices, you have to make connections in your market. Consider going the route of networking, connecting up with, joining the local multifamily group. Word of mouth is powerful.

## [12:13 - 15:14]

Make sure that you protect your reputation in the marketplace. When you get in a deal, close that deal because if you don't, everyone in the marketplace will know you're a jack. A jack is not a good thing, by the way, unless your name is Jack. In which case, by the way, in the real estate game, if your name is Jack, I would definitely change it because they think that you're going to jack them around. Now, on a serious note, what do you need to be doing to create those relationships? You need to make sure the top three brokers in every market that you're shopping in know you are the real deal. How can you prove that in the beginning? Get them a letter of credit from your bank or lender. If you have a bank or lender, Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac, that is willing to give you money, if you have a local regional bank that is willing to give you money, make sure you have that in a letter. If you've done other deals, maybe from another market or the local market, make sure that broker writes a letter saying you are great. If you have done other deals on yourself, make sure sellers say they had a great experience with you. Build your resume so you can go to that broker. If you have money, make sure you show that you have the finances to do a deal without them even asking. I will offer this almost every time to brokers that don't know me. Oh no, no, Grant, I don't need that. I know about you. Good. I'll send it to you anyway. I want to show you they got 40 million sitting in cash in the bank right now and I want to show it to you. Once you're known, you're not going to have to do all that. But even when you're known, you should still bring your troops, show them your ammo, show them your reserves, show them your serious. Remember, continue to be looking daily, collecting data. If Cushman Wakefield or HFF or ARA lost a deal or lost a listing that didn't sell and so the listing expired, it does not mean that that seller is not a seller the next week to you. So you could make contact. You could make a call call. You could introduce yourself. You should pull up the top 100 multifamily owners in the country. I'll be on that list soon if I'm not already. Make sure that you're able to make contact. Owners want to recycle their profits. They want to buy something, make their money on it, deliver returns to their investors and then they're ready to sell again. So understand that you need to know the brokers in the market, the sellers in the market, the on market and off market deals in the market. You need to know the lenders in that market, the banks that are playing in that market and you need to know anybody and everybody that knows anything about multifamily in your market. I take my looking a step further and build a spreadsheet that includes every active deal in that market. Even peer deals, peer, P-E-E-R, deals similar.

## [15:14 - 17:11]

This is my pipeline of deals. Know everything about every deal in your market. When a deal closes, add them to your list. How much it sold for? How much are they renting for? Who got them? How much debt did they use? Who were the buyers? You don't know. They might be sellers a year from now. It's a little extra work but it comes in handy as I bet future opportunities as I sit and wait for maybe somebody to pay too much. I'll tell you about a deal I once did. It was on the coast of Florida. I bought a deal. Actually, I bought eight deals. It was about 1200 units. I had a deal across the street for me going to market. I pursued it. I ran the price up in the bidding war. I knew if it got to a certain level, I was going to let the other guy have the deal because it was going to choke that dude out. He paid too much. I sit and I wait for the day when the economy pulls back and he can no longer function. He can no longer rehab the property, take care of the property, take care of the tenants or take care of the debt. I'll be there sitting while it sits across the street waiting to grab the deal. Know your market. Know the brokers. They're your friends, not your enemies. If you have a real estate license, please don't tell anybody. Nobody cares about your real estate license. What they want to know is this. Do you have money? Can you close? Do you have courage? Are you a closer? Can you get debt? Are you a closer? Do you know how to manage deals? Can you close? Will you close? Will you pay them? Will you be a pain in the ass? Will you be a punk in the clothes? Because if you're like, dude, I'm not Jack. I'm John. Okay? I'm the real deal and I'm a closer. That's what they care about. Not some dumb real estate license that you studied for.
